sourceTitle: Create integrations with the Notion API

questions.question: How do you begin the process of creating an internal integration?

options.text: Go to the Developer Hub and click 'New Integration'
options.explanation: The Developer Hub is for documentation and management tools, but you create the actual integration inside your Notion workspace settings.

options.text: Navigate to Settings > Integrations > Develop your own integrations
options.explanation: This is the correct path. You go to your workspace settings, find the Integrations tab, and click the link to develop your own.

options.text: Go to API Settings and request permission to create an integration
options.explanation: There is no 'API Settings' tab for this. You create integrations directly through the 'Develop your own integrations' link in the sidebar.

options.text: Contact a Workspace owner to request an integration creation
options.explanation: While Workspace owners *can* create integrations, the user can also create them directly if they are an owner, without needing to contact anyone.

questions.question: Who is permitted to access the Integrations tab and create integrations?

options.text: All users with a paid subscription
options.explanation: Access to the Integrations tab and the ability to create integrations is restricted to Workspace owners.

options.text: Workspace owners only
options.explanation: The article explicitly states: 'Only Workspace owners will be able to access the Integrations tab and create integrations.'

options.text: Developers with a Notion Developer account
options.explanation: Creating the integration happens within the workspace itself. A developer account is for documentation and access to public integrations.

options.text: Anyone with a verified email address
options.explanation: Role-based access is required; simply having an email does not grant the ability to create integrations.

questions.question: Why must you ensure you are logged into Notion on your web session with the same account used on desktop?

options.text: To prevent session timeouts while creating the integration
options.explanation: The purpose of this requirement is to ensure the dropdown menu correctly displays the associated workspace.

options.text: To ensure you see the correct account and workspaces in the dropdown menu
options.explanation: The article notes that if you are logged in on web with a different account than desktop, you will not see the correct account and workspaces.

options.text: To enable two-factor authentication for the integration token
options.explanation: Two-factor authentication is a security measure, but not related to the account synchronization requirement for the dropdown menu.

options.text: To verify your IP address matches your registered location
options.explanation: This is a security check, not the reason listed in the article for the account requirement.

questions.question: How can you retrieve the internal integration token?

options.text: It is automatically emailed to the Workspace owner
options.explanation: The token is not emailed; it must be retrieved manually from the integration settings.

options.text: Click the ••• next to the internal integration in Settings > Connections
options.explanation: The token is found by clicking the three dots (•••) next to the integration name in the Connections menu.

options.text: Go to the Developer Hub and generate a new key
options.explanation: The Developer Hub is for documentation and management, but the specific token is retrieved from the workspace's integration settings.

options.text: It is located in the 'Associated workspace' dropdown menu
options.explanation: The dropdown menu is used to select the workspace, not to retrieve the API token.

questions.question: What is true regarding the 'Associated workspace' for an internal integration?

options.text: You can change it at any time via the dropdown menu
options.explanation: Internal integrations are workspace-specific. To change the associated workspace, you must create a new integration.

options.text: You can only select your personal workspace, not a team workspace
options.explanation: You can use the dropdown menu to specify any associated workspace; the restriction is on changing it later.

options.text: Once set, it cannot be changed without creating a new integration
options.explanation: The article explicitly states that to change the associated workspace, you have to create a new integration.

options.text: It defaults to your company's Enterprise workspace
options.explanation: It defaults to the workspace selected in the dropdown during creation, which is determined by your login session.

questions.question: How do you create a public integration?

options.text: Select 'Public' from the dropdown menu during creation
options.explanation: New integrations are created as internal by default. Public integrations must be converted from an internal one.

options.text: Create an internal integration first, then convert it using the developer guide
options.explanation: The article states: 'All newly added integrations are internal initially... first use the above instructions to create an internal integration.'

options.text: You must request permission from Notion Support to make it public
options.explanation: There is no manual approval process; you simply convert the internal integration using the developer guide.

options.text: Public integrations are only available for Enterprise workspaces
options.explanation: Public integrations are a developer feature available to all users who create an internal integration first.

questions.question: Which of the following user capabilities is NOT included in the 'Read user info (including email address)' option?

options.text: User's email address
options.explanation: The 'including email address' option explicitly grants access to the user's email.

options.text: User's profile picture
options.explanation: While not explicitly listed in the 'user capabilities' list, the 'Read user info' capability generally implies access to user details. The key distinction here is the email.

options.text: User's name
options.explanation: The 'Read user info' option includes the user's name.

options.text: User's email address
options.explanation: The 'Read user info (not including user's email address)' option explicitly excludes the email. The version *including* the email would allow access to it.

questions.question: What is a limitation of Integration Webhooks compared to Webhook Actions?

options.text: Integration webhooks cannot monitor activity across multiple workspaces
options.explanation: This is the opposite of the truth. Integration webhooks *can* monitor activity across multiple workspaces.

options.text: Integration webhooks have a limited set of triggers
options.explanation: Webhook actions are designed for simpler use cases with limited triggers; Integration webhooks offer broader capabilities.

options.text: Integration webhooks are designed for simpler use cases like database automations
options.explanation: Webhook actions are designed for simpler use cases. Integration webhooks are for more complex, real-time monitoring across workspaces.

options.text: Integration webhooks can handle an unlimited number of actions
options.explanation: The article states that Integration webhooks can handle an unlimited number of actions, whereas Webhook actions are limited.

questions.question: Which of the following changes does an Integration Webhook NOT support notifications for?

options.text: Database updates
options.explanation: Database updates are supported and are a primary use case for integration webhooks.

options.text: Workspace and teamspace settings changes
options.explanation: The article explicitly lists 'Workspace and teamspace settings changes' as something that Integration Webhooks currently do not support.

options.text: Page content changes
options.explanation: Page content changes are supported and are a primary use case for integration webhooks.

options.text: Webhook action events
options.explanation: Integration webhooks send events to third-party services, which is distinct from 'webhook action events' (which are internal database automations).

questions.question: What is the purpose of Integration Webhooks?

options.text: To manually configure database automations for simple tasks
options.explanation: That describes Webhook Actions. Integration Webhooks are for more advanced real-time monitoring and response.

options.text: To allow integrations to monitor and respond to changes in Notion workspaces in real-time
options.explanation: The article defines Integration Webhooks as enabling integrations to 'monitor and respond to changes in Notion workspaces in real-time'.

options.text: To send automated emails to Workspace owners
options.explanation: Webhooks send data to an endpoint, not emails to users.

options.text: To manage user permissions and access levels
options.explanation: Permissions are managed via the Granular integration permissions settings, not webhooks.

questions.question: Which option represents a valid 'Content capability' that a developer can choose during app setup?

options.text: Manage users
options.explanation: User management is handled under 'User capabilities', not 'Content capabilities'.

options.text: Write content only
options.explanation: The article lists 'Write content only' as one of the valid Content capabilities.

options.text: Delete workspace
options.explanation: This is not a standard capability listed in the article; capabilities are limited to Read, Write, Insert, or User info access.

options.text: Admin access
options.explanation: This is not a granular permission option listed in the article.

questions.question: If you need to be notified about a change in workspace settings, which tool should you use instead of Integration Webhooks?

options.text: Audit log
options.explanation: The article states that for User changes and Workspace/teamspace settings changes, you should use the audit log instead of Integration Webhooks.

options.text: Webhook actions
options.explanation: Webhook actions are designed for database automations and do not support these types of notifications.

options.text: Developer hub
options.explanation: The Developer hub is for documentation and tools, not for receiving notifications about workspace changes.

options.text: Connection settings
options.explanation: Connection settings are for managing the integrations themselves, not for monitoring changes to workspace settings.
